startScreenOptions.title: AN20.1-AN20.9 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 5 embedded questions for AN20.1-AN20.9.

interactions.label: Which anatomical feature of the ankle joint explains why inversion sprains are f

params.question: Which anatomical feature of the ankle joint explains why inversion sprains are far more common than eversion injuries?

answers.text: The lateral ligament is weaker, consisting of three separate bands rather than a single strong fan

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The lateral ligament comprises three discrete bands (ATFL, CFL, PTFL) and is considerably weaker than the deltoid. This structural asymmetry makes the lateral side vulnerable during inversion, with the ATFL tearing first.

answers.text: The medial malleolus extends further distally than the lateral malleolus

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: It is the lateral malleolus that extends further distally and more posteriorly. This actually provides some lateral bony restraint, but the ligamentous weakness still predominates.

answers.text: The joint capsule is thicker on the lateral side, limiting eversion range

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The joint capsule is thin both anteriorly and posteriorly. It thickens into collateral ligaments on each side, but the lateral set is the weaker of the two.

answers.text: The fibula bears more body weight than the tibia

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The fibula bears only 10–15% of body weight. The tibia is the primary weight-bearing bone of the leg.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the ligament asymmetry: the deltoid (medial) ligament is strong and fan-shaped with four parts, while the lateral ligament has three separate, weaker bands — ATFL, CFL, and PTFL.
Correct. The three-band lateral ligament is considerably weaker than the fan-shaped deltoid, making inversion injuries the most common ankle sprain mechanism.

interactions.label: Venous ulcers in chronic venous insufficiency typically form on the lateral aspe

params.question: Venous ulcers in chronic venous insufficiency typically form on the lateral aspect of the ankle, near the lateral malleolus.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Venous ulcers form just above the medial malleolus (the gaiter area), not the lateral ankle. This medial location reflects the anatomy of the great saphenous vein and the point of greatest hydrostatic pressure from perforating vein incompetence.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — the statement is false. Venous ulcers characteristically form just above the medial malleolus, in the gaiter area, where hydrostatic pressure is greatest due to the course of the great saphenous vein and perforating vein incompetence.

interactions.label: Böhler's angle on a lateral calcaneal radiograph normally measures between 20 an

params.question: Böhler's angle on a lateral calcaneal radiograph normally measures between 20 and 40 degrees. What does a reduced Böhler's angle indicate?

answers.text: A calcaneal fracture, typically from axial loading during a fall from height

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A depressed Böhler's angle results from collapse of the posterior facet of the calcaneus, classically caused by a fall from height where axial force crushes the calcaneal body.

answers.text: Ankle joint instability from lateral ligament tears

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Ankle instability is assessed by mortise asymmetry on an AP view, not by Böhler's angle. Ligament tears do not alter calcaneal geometry.

Lisfranc alignment is checked on foot radiographs by confirming that the medial border of the second metatarsal lines up with the medial border of the middle cuneiform — a different measurement entirely.

answers.text: Developmental dysplasia of the hip in an infant

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Developmental dysplasia of the hip is assessed using Hilgenreiner's line through the triradiate cartilages on a pelvis radiograph, not Böhler's angle on the calcaneus.

overallFeedback.feedback: Böhler's angle measures the relationship between the posterior facet and the calcaneal tuberosity. When this angle falls below 20 degrees, suspect a calcaneal fracture — classically from a fall from height.
Correct. A flattened Böhler's angle is a hallmark radiographic sign of calcaneal fracture, most often from a fall landing on the heel.

interactions.label: The popliteal artery is described as the most difficult lower limb pulse to palp

params.question: The popliteal artery is described as the most difficult lower limb pulse to palpate. Which anatomical feature explains this difficulty?

answers.text: It lies deep within the popliteal fossa, covered by overlying soft tissues

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The popliteal artery runs at the deepest point of the popliteal fossa, surrounded by fat and bounded by the hamstrings and gastrocnemius. Firm pressure with the knee slightly flexed is needed to compress it against the posterior surface of the tibia.

answers.text: It has a very small calibre compared to the femoral and tibial arteries

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The popliteal artery is a direct continuation of the femoral artery and maintains a substantial calibre. Its difficulty is due to depth, not diameter.

answers.text: It runs within the interosseous membrane between the tibia and fibula

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The anterior tibial artery passes through the interosseous membrane, not the popliteal artery. The popliteal artery lies posterior to the knee joint.

answers.text: It is compressed by the flexor retinaculum at the ankle

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The flexor retinaculum is at the ankle, forming the roof of the tarsal tunnel. It relates to the posterior tibial artery and tendons, not the popliteal artery behind the knee.

overallFeedback.feedback: The popliteal artery lies at the deepest point of the popliteal fossa behind the knee. Its depth, not its size, makes it the most difficult lower limb pulse to palpate. Flex the knee slightly and press firmly.
Correct. The popliteal artery's deep position within the fossa, surrounded by fat and muscle, makes it the hardest lower limb pulse to feel.

interactions.label: During embryonic development, the lower limb rotates medially by approximately 9

params.question: During embryonic development, the lower limb rotates medially by approximately 90 degrees. Which adult anatomical feature is a direct consequence of this rotation?

answers.text: The dermatomes of the lower limb spiral from anterior to posterior around the limb

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The medial rotation twists the originally straight segmental pattern so that dermatomes wrap in a spiral from the front of the thigh to the back of the leg. This is unique to the lower limb and does not occur in the upper limb, which rotates laterally.

answers.text: The great toe is positioned on the lateral side of the foot

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The great toe ends up on the medial side, not the lateral. It is the developmental equivalent of the thumb, and medial rotation brings it to the medial border.

answers.text: The knee joint functions as a ball-and-socket joint

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The knee is a modified hinge (condylar) joint. The type of joint is determined by articular surface shape, not limb rotation.

answers.text: The hip joint is more prone to dislocation than the shoulder

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The hip joint is actually more stable than the shoulder due to a deeper acetabular socket and strong ligaments. Limb rotation does not make the hip less stable.

overallFeedback.feedback: The lower limb's unique medial rotation during development twists the originally straight segmental nerve pattern into a spiral. This is why lower limb dermatomes wrap around the limb rather than running in neat horizontal bands.
Correct. The 90-degree medial rotation of the lower limb bud spirals the dermatomes from front to back, explaining the characteristic lower limb dermatome map.
